learning_objectives: Define validity, reliability, and fairness in educational measurement
Distinguish between norm-referenced and criterion-referenced assessment
Explain the role of assessment in the teaching-learning cycle
readings: AERA/APA/NCME Standards for Educational and Psychological Testing (overview)

lecture_notes.introduction: Assessment is the bridge between teaching and learning. Good assessment tells us whether learning has occurred, guides instructional decisions, and provides feedback that helps learners improve.
key_concepts: Validity: does the assessment measure what it claims to measure?
Reliability: does the assessment produce consistent results?
Fairness: does the assessment provide equal opportunity to demonstrate learning?
Norm-referenced vs. criterion-referenced interpretations
Formative, interim, and summative assessment purposes
lecture_notes.demonstration: Validity analysis exercise: evaluate three test items for content validity, examining alignment between the item and the stated learning objective
setup_requirements: Sample test items with associated learning objectives

review.value: Not a property of the test itself but of the interpretations drawn from scores; content, construct, and criterion validity provide different types of evidence
Norm-referenced compares students to each other (percentile rank); criterion-referenced measures against a fixed standard (pass/fail threshold)

learning_objectives: Write high-quality multiple-choice items that minimize construct-irrelevant variance
Apply item-writing guidelines to avoid common flaws
Design true/false and matching items for appropriate objectives
readings: Haladyna, T. - Developing and Validating Multiple-Choice Test Items (excerpts)

lecture_notes.introduction: Multiple-choice items are the most widely used assessment format, but most are poorly written. This session teaches the craft of writing items that actually measure understanding, not test-taking skill.
key_concepts: Anatomy of a multiple-choice item: stem, key, distractors
Common item flaws: grammatical cues, absolutes, 'all of the above'
Writing plausible distractors based on common misconceptions
Higher-order MC items: application, analysis, and evaluation
Item analysis: difficulty index and discrimination index
lecture_notes.demonstration: Item repair workshop: identify flaws in 10 poorly written MC items and rewrite them following best practices
setup_requirements: Set of flawed MC items for analysis

review.value: Good distractors represent actual student misconceptions; 'none of the above' and implausible options inflate scores without measuring learning

Difficulty index (proportion correct) and discrimination index (correlation with total score) identify items that are too easy, too hard, or fail to distinguish knowledgeable from unknowledgeable students

learning_objectives: Design essay prompts and short-answer items with clear expectations
Create performance assessments with authentic tasks
Develop analytic and holistic scoring rubrics

lecture_notes.introduction: Selected-response items are efficient but limited. When we want to assess complex thinking, creativity, or real-world skill application, constructed-response and performance assessments are essential.
key_concepts: Short-answer vs. extended-response items
Performance assessment: demonstrations of skill in context
Rubric types: holistic (single overall score) vs. analytic (scored by criteria)
Rubric design: clear criteria, distinct levels, observable indicators
Inter-rater reliability and calibration
lecture_notes.demonstration: Rubric development workshop: design an analytic rubric for a presentation assignment, then calibrate scoring by rating sample student work as a class

review.value: Analytic rubrics provide detailed feedback on multiple criteria; holistic rubrics provide a single overall judgment and are faster to score but less informative
Multiple raters must agree on scores for the rubric to be reliable; calibration sessions where raters discuss anchor papers are essential

learning_objectives: Design formative assessment strategies that inform instructional decisions
Apply principles of effective feedback to promote learning
Implement classroom assessment techniques (CATs) in various settings
readings: Black, P. & Wiliam, D. - Inside the Black Box
Hattie, J. & Timperley, H. - The Power of Feedback

lecture_notes.introduction: Formative assessment is the most powerful tool for improving learning during instruction. When done well, it provides real-time data that allows both teachers and students to adjust their approach.
key_concepts: Black & Wiliam's five key strategies of formative assessment
Feedback that feeds forward: task, process, self-regulation, self levels
Classroom assessment techniques: muddiest point, think-pair-share, exit tickets
Peer assessment and self-assessment as formative tools
Technology-enhanced formative assessment: polling, backchannel, adaptive quizzing
lecture_notes.demonstration: Feedback revision exercise: take three examples of ineffective feedback and rewrite them using Hattie & Timperley's model targeting task and process levels

review.value: Black & Wiliam's research showed that formative assessment produces significant learning gains, with the largest effects for low-achieving students, but only when it changes instruction
Feedback is most effective when it is specific, timely, focused on the task and process (not the person), and provides actionable next steps

learning_objectives: Describe the purposes and limitations of standardized tests
Interpret standardized test scores: percentiles, standard scores, grade equivalents
Evaluate the consequences of high-stakes testing policies

lecture_notes.introduction: Standardized tests are the most visible and controversial form of educational assessment. Understanding what they measure, what they do not measure, and how their results are used and misused is essential for every educator.
key_concepts: Types of standardized tests: achievement, aptitude, diagnostic
Score interpretation: raw scores, percentiles, stanines, standard scores
Grade equivalent scores: the most misinterpreted metric in education
High-stakes consequences: accountability, school ratings, teacher evaluation
Campbell's Law: when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure
lecture_notes.demonstration: Score interpretation exercise: given a standardized test score report, correctly interpret and explain results to a hypothetical parent, avoiding common misinterpretations

review.value: A 3rd grader with a grade equivalent of 6.0 has NOT mastered 6th grade material; the score means the student performed at the level a hypothetical 6th grader would on the 3rd grade test
The more a quantitative social indicator is used for decision-making, the more subject it is to corruption pressures and the more apt it is to distort the processes it is intended to monitor

class_schedule.topic: Program Evaluation and Data-Driven Decision Making
learning_objectives: Distinguish between program evaluation and individual student assessment
Apply logic models to plan program evaluations
Use assessment data to inform program improvement decisions
readings: Fitzpatrick, J. et al. - Program Evaluation Ch. 1-3
W.K. Kellogg Foundation Logic Model Development Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Assessment data is only valuable if it leads to better decisions. Program evaluation provides the framework for systematically collecting and interpreting data about the effectiveness of educational programs, curricula, and interventions.
key_concepts: Program evaluation vs. student assessment: different purposes, different methods
Logic models: inputs, activities, outputs, outcomes, impact
Evaluation approaches: objectives-based, theory-driven, utilization-focused
Mixed methods: combining quantitative and qualitative data
Communicating evaluation findings to stakeholders
lecture_notes.demonstration: Logic model construction: build a logic model for an after-school tutoring program, then identify what data you would collect to evaluate each component
setup_requirements: Sample program description for evaluation planning

review.value: Visual representations of a program's theory of change: if we invest these resources (inputs) and do these things (activities), we expect these products (outputs) leading to these changes (outcomes/impact)
Patton's approach: evaluation should be designed from the start to be useful to the people who will make decisions based on its findings

questions.question: A teacher creates a math test where 70% of the items require reading complex word problems. ESL students score significantly lower than native English speakers, even when they demonstrate equal math ability on computation items. What type of validity threat does this represent, and how should the test be revised?
questions.answer: This represents construct-irrelevant variance — the test is measuring reading comprehension in addition to math ability, which contaminates the validity of inferences about mathematical understanding. Revision: simplify the language of word problems, provide translations or bilingual glossaries, include a mix of computational and word problem items, and consider allowing extra time for ESL students.

rubric.key: Explains how language demands contaminate math measurement

questions.question: Write a high-quality multiple-choice item at the Application level of Bloom's taxonomy for the topic of rubric design. Include one correct answer and three plausible distractors based on common misconceptions.
questions.answer: Item: 'A teacher wants to assess student presentations but needs detailed diagnostic feedback on specific skills. Which rubric type is most appropriate?' A) Holistic rubric with 4 levels (distractor: efficient but lacks specificity). B) Analytic rubric with criteria for content, delivery, visuals, and audience engagement (correct: provides detailed feedback on multiple dimensions). C) Checklist with yes/no indicators (distractor: binary judgments lack nuance). D) Single-point rubric describing only proficient performance (distractor: emerging practice but still less diagnostic than analytic). Justification: Each distractor represents a real assessment tool that students might confuse with the best option.

rubric.key: Item is at Application level, not recall
Correct answer is defensible and clearly best
Distractors are plausible and based on misconceptions

quizzes.topic: Formative Assessment, Standardized Testing, and Evaluation

questions.question: According to Hattie and Timperley's feedback model, feedback operates at four levels: task, process, self-regulation, and self. Why is 'self' level feedback (e.g., 'Good job!' or 'You're so smart!') the least effective for learning?
questions.answer: Self-level feedback (praise directed at the person rather than the work) is least effective because it contains no information about what was done well or how to improve. It can also promote a fixed mindset ('I'm smart') rather than a growth mindset ('I used effective strategies'). Praise feels good but does not transfer to future performance because it does not help the learner understand what actions led to success or what to do differently next time.

rubric.key: Connects to mindset or attribution theory
Contrasts with task or process-level feedback

questions.question: A school district reports that its 4th graders' average standardized test score has increased from the 45th percentile to the 65th percentile over three years. A school board member concludes that instruction has dramatically improved. What alternative explanations should be considered before accepting this conclusion?
questions.answer: Alternative explanations: (1) Teaching to the test — teachers may have narrowed the curriculum to focus on tested content, inflating scores without genuine learning gains (Campbell's Law). (2) Demographic shifts — the student population may have changed due to new housing developments, redistricting, or selective attrition. (3) Test familiarity — students may have become better at the specific test format through repeated practice. (4) Changes in the norming group or test itself across administrations. Without triangulating with other data sources (classroom assessments, course grades, performance tasks), the score increase alone does not prove instructional improvement.

rubric.key: Identifies teaching to the test / Campbell's Law
Identifies at least one other credible alternative
Explains need for multiple data sources to draw valid conclusions

major_assignments.title: Assessment Design Portfolio
major_assignments.description: Create a comprehensive assessment portfolio for a course or training program of your choice. The portfolio must include: (1) a table of specifications mapping objectives to assessment methods, (2) a 10-item selected-response assessment with item analysis data, (3) a performance assessment with analytic rubric, (4) a formative assessment plan with three classroom assessment techniques, and (5) a reflective analysis of alignment and validity across the portfolio.
objectives: Design multiple assessment types aligned to learning objectives
Apply item-writing and rubric-design best practices
Evaluate the validity and reliability of your own assessments
Reflect critically on assessment design decisions
deliverables: 10-item selected-response assessment with answer key and item analysis
Performance assessment task with analytic rubric

criteria: Clear mapping of objectives to assessment types
Analytic rubric with clear criteria and levels
Evaluates validity and alignment across the portfolio
Identifies strengths and limitations of design choices
Demonstrates professional growth in assessment literacy
Comprehensive assessment portfolio with multiple item types
Strong alignment between objectives and assessments

quizzes.description: Bi-weekly quizzes on assessment concepts and item design

lab_assignments.description: Weekly practice exercises: item writing, rubric design, score interpretation

midterm_examination.description: Covers measurement principles, item design, and rubric development

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam emphasizing formative assessment, standardized testing, and program evaluation

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is required for all sessions. Item-writing workshops and calibration exercises cannot be made up. More than 3 absences will reduce the final grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work loses 5% per day for up to 5 days. Portfolio components have staggered deadlines that must be met to receive peer feedback.
course_policies.collaboration: Peer review of assessment items is a course requirement. All submitted work must be individually authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All assessment items and rubrics must be original work. Adapting published items requires proper attribution. Violations result in a zero and academic integrity referral.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Computer with internet access. Spreadsheet software for item analysis calculations. Access to LMS for formative assessment tool exploration.
